title: Application Control and Allowlisting Guide
content:
# Application Control and Allowlisting Guide

## Overview

Application control (also known as application whitelisting or allowlisting) restricts which applications can execute on a system to a pre-approved set. It is one of the most effective security controls against malware, ransomware, and unauthorized software execution. Rather than trying to identify and block known-bad software (blocklisting), application control inverts the model by permitting only known-good software to run.

The Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) ranks application control as one of the Essential Eight mitigation strategies. NIST SP 800-167 specifically addresses application whitelisting guidance. SOC teams must understand how to deploy, monitor, and respond to alerts from application control solutions.

## Application Control Technologies

### Platform-Native Solutions

| Solution | Platform | Capabilities |
|----------|----------|--------------|
| Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) | Windows 10/11, Server 2016+ | Kernel-mode enforcement, code integrity policies, CI policy signing |
| AppLocker | Windows Enterprise/Education | User-mode enforcement, rules by publisher/path/hash, audit mode |
| macOS Gatekeeper + System Policy | macOS | Code signing verification, notarization, quarantine enforcement |
| SELinux / AppArmor | Linux | Mandatory access control, application confinement profiles |
| fapolicyd | RHEL/Fedora | File access policy daemon, trust-based execution control |

### Third-Party Solutions

| Solution | Key Features |
|----------|-------------|
| Carbon Black App Control | Real-time inventory, trust-based policies, change control, file integrity |
| Airlock Digital | Allowlisting with one-time execution (OTP), centralized management |
| ThreatLocker | Zero-trust application control, ringfencing, storage control |
| Ivanti Application Control | Trusted ownership, privilege management, context-aware policies |

## Implementation Strategy

### Phase 1: Discovery and Baselining (Weeks 1-4)

1. **Deploy in audit/monitor mode**

   **WDAC (Windows)**:
   ```powershell
   # Create a WDAC policy from system audit
   New-CIPolicy -FilePath "C:\policies\InitialPolicy.xml" -Level Publisher -Fallback Hash -Audit
   
   # Convert to binary
   ConvertFrom-CIPolicy -XmlFilePath "C:\policies\InitialPolicy.xml" -BinaryFilePath "C:\policies\InitialPolicy.p7b"
   
   # Deploy in audit mode (does not block, only logs)
   # Copy to C:\Windows\System32\CodeIntegrity\CiPolicies\Active\
   ```

   **AppLocker (Windows)**:
   ```powershell
   # Generate default rules
   Get-AppLockerPolicy -Effective | Set-AppLockerPolicy -PolicyObject $_ -Merge
   
   # Set to Audit Only mode
   # Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings >
   # Application Control Policies > AppLocker > Configure rule enforcement
   # Set each collection to "Audit only"
   ```

   **Linux (fapolicyd)**:
   ```bash
   # Install and enable in permissive mode
   sudo dnf install fapolicyd
   sudo systemctl enable fapolicyd
   
   # Configure permissive mode (log only)
   sudo sed -i 's/permissive = 0/permissive = 1/' /etc/fapolicyd/fapolicyd.conf
   sudo systemctl start fapolicyd
   
   # Review audit log
   sudo fapolicyd-cli --dump-db
   ```

2. **Collect application inventory**

   ```powershell
   # Windows: Collect installed applications
   Get-ItemProperty HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\* |
     Select-Object DisplayName, DisplayVersion, Publisher, InstallDate |
     Export-Csv -Path "C:\audit\installed_apps.csv"
   
   # Collect running processes with paths and hashes
   Get-Process | ForEach-Object {
     $path = $_.Path
     if ($path) {
       $hash = (Get-FileHash -Path $path -Algorithm SHA256 -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).Hash
       [PSCustomObject]@{
         Name = $_.ProcessName
         Path = $path
         SHA256 = $hash
         Publisher = (Get-AuthenticodeSignature -FilePath $path).SignerCertificate.Subject
       }
     }
   } | Export-Csv -Path "C:\audit\running_processes.csv"
   ```

3. **Analyze audit logs for blocked applications**

   ```powershell
   # WDAC audit events (Event ID 3076 = audit block)
   Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity/Operational'; Id=3076} |
     Select-Object TimeCreated, Message | Export-Csv "C:\audit\wdac_audit.csv"
   
   # AppLocker audit events (Event IDs 8003, 8006)
   Get-WinEvent -FilterHashtable @{LogName='Microsoft-Windows-AppLocker/EXE and DLL'; Id=8003,8004} |
     Select-Object TimeCreated, Message | Export-Csv "C:\audit\applocker_audit.csv"
   ```

### Phase 2: Policy Development (Weeks 5-8)

1. **Define trust levels**

   | Trust Level | Description | Policy Action |
   |------------|-------------|---------------|
   | Publisher (highest trust) | Software signed by a trusted publisher | Allow all versions from publisher |
   | Publisher + Version | Software signed by trusted publisher, minimum version | Allow specific version and above |
   | File Hash | Specific file identified by cryptographic hash | Allow exact file only |
   | Path (lowest trust) | Any executable in a specified directory | Allow all files in path (use cautiously) |

2. **Create tiered policies**

   | Policy Tier | Scope | Trust Method | Maintenance |
   |------------|-------|-------------|-------------|
   | Base policy | All systems | Publisher rules for Microsoft, OS components | Low -- updated with OS updates |
   | Department policy | Department-specific | Publisher rules for department applications | Medium -- updated with new software |
   | Exception policy | Individual systems | Hash rules for unsigned or unique software | High -- manual hash updates |

3. **WDAC policy creation example**:

   ```powershell
   # Create base policy allowing Microsoft-signed code
   New-CIPolicy -FilePath "C:\policies\BasePolicy.xml" -Level Publisher -Fallback Hash
   
   # Add trusted publishers
   Add-SignerRule -FilePath "C:\policies\BasePolicy.xml" -CertificatePath "C:\certs\vendor.cer" -User
   
   # Merge multiple policies
   Merge-CIPolicy -PolicyPaths "C:\policies\BasePolicy.xml","C:\policies\DeptPolicy.xml" -OutputFilePath "C:\policies\MergedPolicy.xml"
   
   # Set to enforced mode
   Set-RuleOption -FilePath "C:\policies\MergedPolicy.xml" -Option 3 -Delete
   # Option 3 = Audit Mode; deleting it enables enforcement
   ```

### Phase 3: Enforcement Rollout (Weeks 9-12)

1. **Staged rollout plan**

   | Stage | Scope | Duration | Success Criteria |
   |-------|-------|----------|------------------|
   | Pilot | 5-10 systems (IT/Security team) | 2 weeks | <5 false positives per day |
   | Ring 1 | 50 systems (early adopters) | 2 weeks | <10 total false positives per day |
   | Ring 2 | 25% of fleet (low-risk groups) | 2 weeks | <1 false positive per system per week |
   | Ring 3 | 50% of fleet | 2 weeks | Stable, exceptions process functional |
   | Full deployment | 100% of fleet | Ongoing | Continuous monitoring |

2. **Emergency bypass procedures**

   ```
   User reports application blocked
     |
     +-- Is it a known, approved application?
     |   |
     |   +-- YES --> Hash was likely updated (application update)
     |   |           Generate new hash rule
     |   |           Deploy updated policy within 4 hours
     |   |
     |   +-- NO --> Is it a legitimate business need?
     |       |
     |       +-- YES --> Create exception request
     |       |           Manager approval required
     |       |           Security review required
     |       |           Add to policy after approval
     |       |
     |       +-- NO --> Deny request, investigate why user attempted execution
     |
     +-- Is it urgent (business-critical blocking)?
         |
         +-- YES --> Escalate to security team for emergency exception
         |           Temporary hash-based exception (24-hour expiry)
         |           Full review within 24 hours
         |
         +-- NO --> Standard exception request process (SLA: 4 hours)
   ```

## SOC Monitoring

### Application Control Event IDs

**WDAC Events (Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity/Operational)**:

| Event ID | Description | SOC Action |
|----------|-------------|------------|
| 3076 | Audit mode: Code integrity determined a process attempted to load a file that did not meet policy | Review -- potential rule gap or unauthorized software |
| 3077 | Enforce mode: Code integrity determined a process attempted to load a file that did not meet policy | Investigate -- blocked execution, verify legitimate or malicious |
| 3089 | Signature verification of file under validation failed | Investigate -- possible tampering or corruption |
| 3099 | Policy refresh event | Informational -- policy was updated |

**AppLocker Events**:

| Event ID | Log | Description | SOC Action |
|----------|-----|-------------|------------|
| 8003 | EXE and DLL | File was allowed to run (audit mode) | Baseline analysis |
| 8004 | EXE and DLL | File was prevented from running | Investigate block |
| 8005 | EXE and DLL | File was allowed to run | Informational |
| 8006 | EXE and DLL | File would have been prevented (audit mode) | Review for policy tuning |
| 8007 | MSI and Script | File was prevented from running | Investigate block |

### Detection Rules

```
# Alert: Repeated blocked execution attempts (possible malware)
WDAC_Events
WHERE EventID == 3077
GROUP BY Computer, FilePath
HAVING count > 5 within 1 hour
--> P2: Multiple blocked execution attempts, investigate for malware

# Alert: Blocked execution from suspicious directory
WDAC_Events
WHERE EventID == 3077
AND (FilePath LIKE "%\Temp\%" OR FilePath LIKE "%\AppData\%"
     OR FilePath LIKE "%\Downloads\%" OR FilePath LIKE "%\ProgramData\%")
--> P2: Blocked execution from user-writable directory

# Alert: Application control policy disabled
System_Events
WHERE Source == "Microsoft-Windows-CodeIntegrity"
AND (Message CONTAINS "policy disabled" OR Message CONTAINS "audit mode")
--> P1: Application control enforcement may have been disabled

# Alert: Blocked execution of known attack tool
WDAC_Events
WHERE EventID == 3077
AND FileName IN ("mimikatz.exe", "psexec.exe", "cobalt", "beacon",
    "rubeus.exe", "sharphound.exe", "bloodhound", "lazagne.exe")
--> P1: Known attack tool execution blocked
```

### Investigation Workflow

When an application control block occurs:

1. **Identify the blocked file**
   - File path, name, hash (SHA-256)
   - Code signing status (signed/unsigned, publisher)
   - File creation timestamp (when was it written to disk?)

2. **Determine origin**
   - How did the file arrive? (download, email, USB, network share)
   - Check browser download history, email logs, file copy events
   - Was it downloaded by the user or dropped by another process?

3. **Assess legitimacy**
   - Is the publisher known and trusted?
   - Is this a known application used in the organization?
   - Does the file hash match known-good versions?
   - Submit hash to VirusTotal or sandbox if unknown

4. **Take action**
   - Legitimate + approved: Update allowlist policy
   - Legitimate + unapproved: Route through software request process
   - Suspicious/malicious: Initiate malware investigation workflow

## Common Implementation Challenges

1. **Application updates breaking hash rules** -- Publisher-based rules are more resilient than hash rules. Use publisher rules as the primary trust method and reserve hash rules for unsigned software.

2. **Unsigned internal applications** -- Code-sign internal applications with an organizational certificate and create publisher rules for that certificate.

3. **Script execution control** -- WDAC can control PowerShell in Constrained Language Mode. AppLocker can control script execution. Both require careful policy to avoid breaking administrative workflows.

4. **DLL control performance impact** -- Enabling DLL rules in AppLocker or WDAC can cause performance overhead. Test thoroughly before enabling in production.

5. **User resistance** -- Communicate the rollout plan, provide clear exception request process, and ensure IT support can resolve blocks quickly.

## MITRE ATT&CK Relevance

Application control mitigates the following techniques:

| Technique | ID | How Application Control Helps |
|-----------|----|-----------------------|
| User Execution | T1204 | Blocks unauthorized executables even if user attempts to run them |
| Command and Scripting Interpreter | T1059 | Constrains script engines to approved scripts |
| Signed Binary Proxy Execution | T1218 | Can restrict LOLBin abuse when properly configured |
| Software Deployment Tools | T1072 | Blocks unauthorized software distribution |
| Ingress Tool Transfer | T1105 | Downloaded tools cannot execute without policy approval |
| Native API | T1106 | WDAC kernel-mode enforcement prevents unsigned code execution |

## References

- NIST SP 800-167: Guide to Application Whitelisting
- ASD Essential Eight: Application Control
- Microsoft WDAC Documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/application-security/application-control/windows-defender-application-control/
- Microsoft AppLocker Documentation: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/security/application-security/application-control/windows-defender-application-control/applocker/applocker-overview
- NSA Application Allowlisting Guide
